messages.content:
You paraphrase a research PROBLEM STATEMENT across 7 ML domains.
Your output is a *domain-grounded problem description* — written in
the target domain's natural research vocabulary, but stating only
the failure mode, NOT a proposed solution.

## Two-step procedure
1. Extract the FAILURE MODE from the input query: the underlying
   gap, bottleneck, or constraint. Strip out any solution direction
   the query may already suggest. Express the failure mode as one
   abstract sentence (the "essence").
2. For each domain, describe an analogous failure mode in that
   domain — using *3-5 domain-specific technical terms* so the
   paraphrase reads like a problem statement an actual researcher
   in that domain would write in their paper's introduction.

## Strict rules — follow exactly
- Use PROBLEM vocabulary: "X is slow when ...", "Y fails when ...",
  "Z is costly because ...", "W is unavailable but needed for ..."
- DO NOT use SOLUTION vocabulary: NO "use ... to ...", NO "build ...
  that ...", NO "apply ... for ...", NO "design a ... which ...".
- DO NOT name a specific algorithmic *solution* (e.g. "two-stage
  cascade", "Gumbel-Softmax sampling"). The paraphrase describes a
  problem; the analogous mechanisms will be discovered downstream.
- DO use domain-specific *concepts and quantities* that bound the
  failure mode (e.g. "candidate pool", "feature map resolution",
  "rollout horizon", "phoneme alignment") — these anchor the
  problem in the domain's natural research vocabulary.
- The paraphrase MUST end as a problem statement, not a solution
  prescription.

## Domain vocabulary anchors (use 3-5 per paraphrase, NOT exhaustive)
  LLM_NLP    : token, decoding step, KV cache, context length,
               attention head, perplexity, in-context examples
  MULTIMODAL : visual token, image embedding, cross-modal alignment,
               query-conditioned attention, caption, VQA accuracy
  CV         : feature map, patch token, spatial resolution,
               receptive field, anchor box, segmentation mask
  RL         : policy, value function, rollout, reward signal,
               trajectory, exploration, sample efficiency
  IR_REC     : candidate pool, query-document score, reranking,
               cross-encoder, recall@k, embedding index
  SPEECH     : acoustic frame, phoneme, spectrogram, alignment,
               WER, streaming latency, vocoder
  ROBOTICS   : sensorimotor loop, manipulation policy, dynamics
               model, motion plan, sim-to-real, contact reasoning

Push hard — even cross-domain mappings that *feel* forced often
surface useful seeds when essence-aligned. Only emit `null` for a
domain when the failure mode genuinely has no analog there.

## Reference example
  Input query: "Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token
                needs a full large-model forward pass; how can a small
                draft model propose tokens in parallel that the large
                model verifies without quality loss?"

  GOOD essence: "Sequential single-step inference incurs O(N)
                 expensive forward passes when N items must be
                 decided in order, even when many decisions could
                 be made in parallel."

  GOOD IR_REC paraphrase: "In information retrieval, cross-encoder
                            reranking of a candidate pool requires a
                            forward pass per (query, document) pair;
                            recall@k depends on this expensive
                            scoring step yet most candidates could
                            be filtered with cheaper signals before
                            final ranking."
                            ↑ uses: cross-encoder, candidate pool,
                                     query-document scoring, recall@k,
                                     reranking — IR vocabulary

  GOOD CV paraphrase:      "In dense visual perception, per-patch
                            token processing through a heavy encoder
                            dominates inference latency for high
                            spatial resolution inputs; segmentation
                            quality depends on this dense scoring
                            even though most patches are background
                            or low-saliency."
                            ↑ uses: patch token, spatial resolution,
                                     segmentation, dense scoring

  BAD IR_REC paraphrase (DO NOT produce — solution-form):
                          "Build a two-stage ranker where a cheap
                           encoder proposes candidates and a
                           cross-encoder verifies accepted spans."

  BAD IR_REC paraphrase (DO NOT produce — too abstract):
                          "Relevance estimation is sequential and
                           costly when each item must be scored in
                           turn; quality must not degrade under
                           cheaper alternatives."
                           ↑ no IR-specific vocabulary, would
                             match poorly against real IR seeds

Always produce the GOOD form: domain-vocabulary-rich problem
statement.

Output JSON:
  {
    "essence":  "<one sentence stating the abstract failure mode>",
    "paraphrases": {
      "LLM_NLP":    "<domain-grounded problem statement>",
      "MULTIMODAL": "...",
      "CV":         "...",
      "RL":         "...",
      "IR_REC":     "...",
      "SPEECH":     "...",
      "ROBOTICS":   null
    }
  }

Query: Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?

You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed 5123915b5345 [SPEECH] — "Branchformer: Parallel MLP-Attention Architectures to Capture Local and Global Context for Speech Recognition and Understanding"
- Retrieved via: Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?

- Problem: Deployed ASR/SLU systems often need variable inference complexity/speed without maintaining multiple models or retraining.
- Method:  Branchformer applies branch dropout to the attention branch during training so the same trained model can run in two inference modes: (1) both branches for higher accuracy, or (2) a pruned cgMLP-only path with linear complexity. This requires no fine-tuning, reduces encoder forward time to linear scaling, and the pruned model achieves performance comparable to a standalone cgMLP trained from scratch.

### Seed ff2cf1c24dba [MULTIMODAL] — "You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model"
- Retrieved via: Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?

- Problem: Unified vision-language (encoder-decoder) models incur high inference cost and existing early-exit methods either target encoder-only models or only the decoder, since estimating encoder-side confidence is hard; this leads to suboptimal compute savings.
- Method:  MuE introduces dynamic early exiting in both encoder and decoder by using layer-wise input similarity as the exit signal. It measures cosine similarity of hidden states across layers to detect saturation; when similarity exceeds a threshold, subsequent layers are skipped. This multiple-exit mechanism applies per decoding step (with a decayed threshold for autoregressive generation) and achieves large speedups (e.g., up to ~50% expected time reduction on SNLI-VE and ~40% on COCO) with minimal performance drop.

### Seed 174a1ef09924 [IR_REC] — "Train Once, Deploy Anywhere: Matryoshka Representation Learning for Multimodal Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?

- Problem: Training multi-granularity recommendation models independently imposes substantial memory and compute overhead for parameters and activations.
- Method:  By embedding smaller weights and activations into larger ones via the fMRLRec linear transformation and computing all slices with a single masked matrix multiplication, fMRLRec avoids training separate models. Theoretical analysis shows about 33% savings in parameters and activations compared to independently training each size, and empirical results confirm convergence toward this savings across target sizes. (Supported by: lines 26–31, 171–187, 241–242).

### Seed d919f55daf69 [MULTIMODAL] — "ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision"
- Retrieved via: In vision–language models, inference is slow because query-conditioned attention computes full cross-modal alignment between all visual tokens and text embeddings, causing latency to scale with image embedding size even when only a few regions are salient for VQA accuracy.
- Problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
- Method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

### Seed 7f79d5d52bbc [MULTIMODAL] — "VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation"
- Retrieved via: In vision–language models, inference is slow because query-conditioned attention computes full cross-modal alignment between all visual tokens and text embeddings, causing latency to scale with image embedding size even when only a few regions are salient for VQA accuracy.
- Problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
- Method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

### Seed d54cefc9ff77 [MULTIMODAL] — "Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training"
- Retrieved via: In vision–language models, inference is slow because query-conditioned attention computes full cross-modal alignment between all visual tokens and text embeddings, causing latency to scale with image embedding size even when only a few regions are salient for VQA accuracy.
- Problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
- Method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

### Seed 57d98ad1e6bc [CV] — "ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer"
- Retrieved via: Dense visual backbones are inefficient because they generate high-resolution feature maps by processing every patch token with a large receptive field, so runtime grows with spatial resolution even though segmentation masks and detections depend on sparse foreground structure.
- Problem: Designing a general-purpose vision backbone that balances accuracy and efficiency across classification, detection, and segmentation while modeling long-range dependencies without deep stacking.
- Method:  ScalableViT is a hierarchical ViT backbone that alternates IW-MSA and SSA blocks within each stage and inserts a positional encoding generator (PEG) at the front of stages. This topology leverages SSA’s context-preserving scaling and IWSA’s inter-window interaction to capture long-range dependencies in single layers, improving the accuracy–cost trade-off. Empirically, ScalableViT-S improves ImageNet-1K top-1 by +1.4% over Twins-SVT-S and +1.8% over Swin-T at similar FLOPs, and yields substantial gains on COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation.

### Seed 2f7eefc7a9b8 [ROBOTICS] — "ConceptGraphs : Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graphs for Perception and Planning"
- Retrieved via: Dense visual backbones are inefficient because they generate high-resolution feature maps by processing every patch token with a large receptive field, so runtime grows with spatial resolution even though segmentation masks and detections depend on sparse foreground structure.
- Problem: Dense per-point 3D maps that fuse 2D foundation model features are memory-inefficient, hard to maintain, and lack structure; robots need scalable, open-vocabulary scene representations built online from RGB-D.
- Method:  ConceptGraphs performs object-centric 3D mapping by running class-agnostic 2D segmentation (e.g., SAM), extracting per-mask CLIP/DINO embeddings, projecting masked regions to 3D, and associating detections across views using a combined geometric nearest-neighbor overlap and semantic (normalized cosine) similarity with greedy assignment. Matched detections are fused by averaging features and merging/downsampling point clouds to form compact object nodes, enabling open-vocabulary reasoning without any 3D training/finetuning and supporting efficient, dynamic updates with a low memory footprint.

### Seed d57567addec9 [CV] — "Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models"
- Retrieved via: Dense visual backbones are inefficient because they generate high-resolution feature maps by processing every patch token with a large receptive field, so runtime grows with spatial resolution even though segmentation masks and detections depend on sparse foreground structure.
- Problem: Next-patch pixel prediction can force the backbone to specialize in low-level details, hurting the quality of transferable features.
- Method:  Insert a heavily-parameterized token-level MLP prediction head atop the ViT trunk that processes each patch independently before projecting to pixels. This head captures low-level signals needed for accurate pixel regression, keeping the trunk focused on higher-level, transferable representations. Experiments show the MLP head significantly improves downstream performance over having no head, a transformer head brings only marginal gains at much higher cost, and increasing head depth (more MLP blocks) is especially beneficial.

### Seed 6b2ca65df0b4 [LLM_NLP] — "SPPD: Self-training with Process Preference Learning Using Dynamic Value Margin"
- Retrieved via: In online control, the policy and value function incur full forward passes at every step, inflating action latency and limiting rollout throughput across long trajectories, even when the reward signal is determined by a small subset of state features.
- Problem: There is a lack of theoretical grounding connecting offline step-level preference optimization to online reinforcement learning, limiting confidence in stability and policy improvement.
- Method:  Define a preference decoding model π_pθ over binary branches and show that, under a specific reward r(τ)=∏_t π_ref(a_t|s_t)/π_pθ(a_t|s_t), the gradient of an offline every-step preference loss equals the on-policy policy gradient of π_pθ. This establishes an equivalence between offline step-DPO-style optimization and on-policy policy gradient under reward constraints, justifying SPPD’s self-training procedure.

### Seed a77ab0ca7966 [IR_REC] — "Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems"
- Retrieved via: In online control, the policy and value function incur full forward passes at every step, inflating action latency and limiting rollout throughput across long trajectories, even when the reward signal is determined by a small subset of state features.
- Problem: Purely offline batch RL for fusion-weight optimization is constrained by behavior-policy coverage, risking local optima and persistent extrapolation issues in the continuous action space.
- Method:  Introduce an online exploration module that gathers trajectories with a Mixed Multi-Exploration policy, combining random exploration and Gaussian action-noise around the current target policy’s actions. This enriches coverage with high-value, near-optimal actions while maintaining diversity, and the collected data are fed back into offline training—improving robustness and outperforming random-only exploration.

### Seed c0f89de47a61 [RL] — "Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL"
- Retrieved via: In online control, the policy and value function incur full forward passes at every step, inflating action latency and limiting rollout throughput across long trajectories, even when the reward signal is determined by a small subset of state features.
- Problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
- Method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

### Seed 80c6fcc2f5d6 [IR_REC] — "Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval"
- Retrieved via: Cross-encoder reranking is compute-heavy because each (query, document) in the candidate pool is scored with the entire stack, so cost scales with candidate pool size and sequence length even when only a few passages dominate the query-document score and recall@k.
- Problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
- Method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

### Seed cdb65a898b67 [IR_REC] — "FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding"
- Retrieved via: Cross-encoder reranking is compute-heavy because each (query, document) in the candidate pool is scored with the entire stack, so cost scales with candidate pool size and sequence length even when only a few passages dominate the query-document score and recall@k.
- Problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
- Method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

### Seed 57b1e475a15e [IR_REC] — "Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: Cross-encoder reranking is compute-heavy because each (query, document) in the candidate pool is scored with the entire stack, so cost scales with candidate pool size and sequence length even when only a few passages dominate the query-document score and recall@k.
- Problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
- Method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

### Seed 38b3a429da83 [SPEECH] — "Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers"
- Retrieved via: Streaming ASR is inefficient because the model processes every acoustic frame of the spectrogram with the full stack, increasing streaming latency even though extended regions (e.g., silence) contribute little to phoneme likelihoods or alignment.
- Problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
- Method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

### Seed 1905468c9d3f [SPEECH] — "RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings"
- Retrieved via: Streaming ASR is inefficient because the model processes every acoustic frame of the spectrogram with the full stack, increasing streaming latency even though extended regions (e.g., silence) contribute little to phoneme likelihoods or alignment.
- Problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
- Method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

### Seed b63494f09d1d [SPEECH] — "UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data"
- Retrieved via: Streaming ASR is inefficient because the model processes every acoustic frame of the spectrogram with the full stack, increasing streaming latency even though extended regions (e.g., silence) contribute little to phoneme likelihoods or alignment.
- Problem: Low-resource ASR lacks labeled data, while purely supervised transfer ignores abundant unlabeled target audio and purely self-supervised pretraining may not learn phoneme-aligned, SR-specific features that transfer well across languages and domains.
- Method:  Pretrain a wav2vec 2.0-style encoder with a unified multitask objective that combines supervised phonetic CTC and phonetically-aware contrastive self-supervision. On labeled source data, jointly optimize CTC to align contextual representations with phoneme labels and a masked contrastive loss between contextual embeddings and quantized latent targets; on unlabeled target data, apply the same contrastive objective to adapt the codebook and contextual space. The architecture uses a convolutional feature encoder, a Transformer context network, and a Gumbel-softmax vector quantizer with codebook diversity regularization, followed by fine-tuning (freezing the feature encoder) on small labeled target sets.

### Seed 2bb16077137f [ROBOTICS] — "Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs"
- Retrieved via: In real-time manipulation, the sensorimotor loop executes a monolithic manipulation policy and dynamics model at every control cycle, making control latency scale with the full network even when contact reasoning and the motion plan depend on a localized subset of state and actuators.
- Problem: Robotic policies must fuse asynchronous, multi-frequency sensory streams (e.g., sparse images with high-rate proprioception/forces) and act nearly in continuous time. Common approaches batch observations into fixed windows or use open-loop primitives, failing to capture fast in-between dynamics and temporal structure.
- Method:  Introduce InFuser, a hybrid continuous-time policy that treats control as the solution of a Neural Controlled Differential Equation. The model conditions on an image embedding and is driven by the higher-frequency sensor stream to evolve a continuous-time multisensory latent state, from which actions are decoded at any intermediate time. The initial action uses only currently available measurements, while subsequent actions integrate new high-rate observations via the CDE. Training uses adjoint-based backpropagation and behavior cloning over control functions. The architecture includes passing high-frequency measurements as a subvector of the latent and stacking an identity block in the CDE vector field, and it generalizes ODE/RNN/DMP parameterizations.

### Seed 67990031a190 [ROBOTICS] — "Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks"
- Retrieved via: In real-time manipulation, the sensorimotor loop executes a monolithic manipulation policy and dynamics model at every control cycle, making control latency scale with the full network even when contact reasoning and the motion plan depend on a localized subset of state and actuators.
- Problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
- Method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

### Seed ee85a0e0bcbc [ROBOTICS] — "Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning"
- Retrieved via: In real-time manipulation, the sensorimotor loop executes a monolithic manipulation policy and dynamics model at every control cycle, making control latency scale with the full network even when contact reasoning and the motion plan depend on a localized subset of state and actuators.
- Problem: Many manipulation tasks depend on multiple objects whose relative positions matter, requiring perception that can jointly localize several task-relevant items and feed a compact state to the policy.
- Method:  Stack multiple attention vectors in W to attend to several objects simultaneously over the shared meta-attention proposals and semantic features. Learn these attentions from a small set of kinesthetic demonstrations (initialized with example crops), then feed the attended boxes to a guided policy search controller. Demonstrated on a sweeping task (orange into dustpan) with varying object positions, achieving strong performance and robustness to distractors.

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?


### Proposed method
We propose Conditional Subnetwork Activation (CSA), a single model trained to expose multiple, input-dependent compute paths at inference. Each backbone block contains two parallel branches: a light, local-processing path (e.g., gated MLP or depthwise-conv MLP) and a heavier, global-context path (e.g., attention). A tiny router (shared across tokens or operating on token groups) chooses which branch(es) to execute per block given a cheap summary of the current hidden state. During training, we apply stochastic branch dropout to force both branches to be individually competent and to encourage graceful degradation when only one is active. We add a soft FLOPs budget regularizer on the router’s expected choices so the model learns to allocate compute where it pays off most.

To avoid running unnecessary depth, we attach auxiliary prediction heads at several layers and adopt a dynamic early-exit rule driven by layer-wise saturation: compute the cosine similarity between consecutive hidden states; when similarity exceeds a calibrated threshold and the corresponding head’s confidence is sufficient, we stop and emit the output. For autoregressive generation, we decay the threshold across decoding steps and cap minimum layers to avoid premature exits on early tokens. This turns depth into an on-demand resource, activating more layers only for difficult inputs or steps.

Within each block, we further make width conditional by nesting smaller channel slices inside larger ones. Linear and attention projections are parameterized in a Matryoshka style so the first w% of channels approximate a competent subnetwork and larger widths strictly extend it. We implement this as masked matrix multiplications/grouped projections that compute only the selected slice, avoiding kernel recompilations. Training samples widths per batch (e.g., 25/50/100%), distills from the full-width path to narrower slices, and jointly optimizes the task loss so all widths remain aligned.

At inference, CSA follows a progressive schedule that minimizes overhead: start with a narrow width and only the light branch for a small number of layers; if the saturation metric indicates underfit or the head is uncertain, escalate compute by enabling the heavy branch and/or increasing width for subsequent layers; otherwise, early-exit. Routers operate with hysteresis and a global budget controller to stabilize decisions and respect latency targets. Optionally, a lightweight token scorer can route only the top-p tokens through the heavy branch while the remainder stay on the light path, providing additional sparsity without architectural changes.

### Rationale
Dense models waste compute by executing the full depth, width, and global-context machinery on every input. CSA reduces all three dimensions adaptively: branch routing decides whether to pay for global context per block; early exiting truncates depth once representations saturate; and Matryoshka-style slicing executes only the necessary width. Because branches are trained with dropout and slices are co-trained with distillation, any activated subnetwork remains well-formed, limiting accuracy loss under reduced compute.

The key is to couple low-cost, reliable signals to gating. Layer-wise cosine similarity provides a model-internal saturation proxy that generalizes across tasks and supports safe early exit. Branch dropout makes per-branch activation robust, enabling per-input routing without retraining. Nested weight slicing turns width into a knob the router can adjust on-the-fly using the same backbone, avoiding multiple models. Together, these mechanisms conditionally activate only the relevant subnetwork for each input and deliver substantial inference savings with bounded quality impact.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [SPEECH] Branchformer: Parallel MLP-Attention Architectures to Capture Local and Global Context for Speech Recognition and Understanding (2207.02971)
   problem: Deployed ASR/SLU systems often need variable inference complexity/speed without maintaining multiple models or retraining.
   method:  Branchformer applies branch dropout to the attention branch during training so the same trained model can run in two inference modes: (1) both branches for higher accuracy, or (2) a pruned cgMLP-only path with linear complexity. This requires no fine-tuning, reduces encoder forward time to linear scaling, and the pruned model achieves performance comparable to a standalone cgMLP trained from scratch.

2. [MULTIMODAL] You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model (2211.11152)
   problem: Unified vision-language (encoder-decoder) models incur high inference cost and existing early-exit methods either target encoder-only models or only the decoder, since estimating encoder-side confidence is hard; this leads to suboptimal compute savings.
   method:  MuE introduces dynamic early exiting in both encoder and decoder by using layer-wise input similarity as the exit signal. It measures cosine similarity of hidden states across layers to detect saturation; when similarity exceeds a threshold, subsequent layers are skipped. This multiple-exit mechanism applies per decoding step (with a decayed threshold for autoregressive generation) and achieves large speedups (e.g., up to ~50% expected time reduction on SNLI-VE and ~40% on COCO) with minimal performance drop.

3. [IR_REC] Train Once, Deploy Anywhere: Matryoshka Representation Learning for Multimodal Recommendation (2409.16627)
   problem: Training multi-granularity recommendation models independently imposes substantial memory and compute overhead for parameters and activations.
   method:  By embedding smaller weights and activations into larger ones via the fMRLRec linear transformation and computing all slices with a single masked matrix multiplication, fMRLRec avoids training separate models. Theoretical analysis shows about 33% savings in parameters and activations compared to independently training each size, and empirical results confirm convergence toward this savings across target sizes. (Supported by: lines 26–31, 171–187, 241–242).

4. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

5. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

7. [CV] ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer (2203.10790)
   problem: Designing a general-purpose vision backbone that balances accuracy and efficiency across classification, detection, and segmentation while modeling long-range dependencies without deep stacking.
   method:  ScalableViT is a hierarchical ViT backbone that alternates IW-MSA and SSA blocks within each stage and inserts a positional encoding generator (PEG) at the front of stages. This topology leverages SSA’s context-preserving scaling and IWSA’s inter-window interaction to capture long-range dependencies in single layers, improving the accuracy–cost trade-off. Empirically, ScalableViT-S improves ImageNet-1K top-1 by +1.4% over Twins-SVT-S and +1.8% over Swin-T at similar FLOPs, and yields substantial gains on COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation.

8. [ROBOTICS] ConceptGraphs : Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graphs for Perception and Planning (2309.16650)
   problem: Dense per-point 3D maps that fuse 2D foundation model features are memory-inefficient, hard to maintain, and lack structure; robots need scalable, open-vocabulary scene representations built online from RGB-D.
   method:  ConceptGraphs performs object-centric 3D mapping by running class-agnostic 2D segmentation (e.g., SAM), extracting per-mask CLIP/DINO embeddings, projecting masked regions to 3D, and associating detections across views using a combined geometric nearest-neighbor overlap and semantic (normalized cosine) similarity with greedy assignment. Matched detections are fused by averaging features and merging/downsampling point clouds to form compact object nodes, enabling open-vocabulary reasoning without any 3D training/finetuning and supporting efficient, dynamic updates with a low memory footprint.

9. [CV] Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models (2401.08541)
   problem: Next-patch pixel prediction can force the backbone to specialize in low-level details, hurting the quality of transferable features.
   method:  Insert a heavily-parameterized token-level MLP prediction head atop the ViT trunk that processes each patch independently before projecting to pixels. This head captures low-level signals needed for accurate pixel regression, keeping the trunk focused on higher-level, transferable representations. Experiments show the MLP head significantly improves downstream performance over having no head, a transformer head brings only marginal gains at much higher cost, and increasing head depth (more MLP blocks) is especially beneficial.

10. [LLM_NLP] SPPD: Self-training with Process Preference Learning Using Dynamic Value Margin (2502.13516)
   problem: There is a lack of theoretical grounding connecting offline step-level preference optimization to online reinforcement learning, limiting confidence in stability and policy improvement.
   method:  Define a preference decoding model π_pθ over binary branches and show that, under a specific reward r(τ)=∏_t π_ref(a_t|s_t)/π_pθ(a_t|s_t), the gradient of an offline every-step preference loss equals the on-policy policy gradient of π_pθ. This establishes an equivalence between offline step-DPO-style optimization and on-policy policy gradient under reward constraints, justifying SPPD’s self-training procedure.

11. [IR_REC] Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems (2208.04560)
   problem: Purely offline batch RL for fusion-weight optimization is constrained by behavior-policy coverage, risking local optima and persistent extrapolation issues in the continuous action space.
   method:  Introduce an online exploration module that gathers trajectories with a Mixed Multi-Exploration policy, combining random exploration and Gaussian action-noise around the current target policy’s actions. This enriches coverage with high-value, near-optimal actions while maintaining diversity, and the collected data are fed back into offline training—improving robustness and outperforming random-only exploration.

12. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

13. [IR_REC] Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval (2203.08372)
   problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
   method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

14. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

15. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

16. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
   method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

17. [SPEECH] RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings (2203.07172)
   problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
   method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

18. [SPEECH] UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data (2101.07597)
   problem: Low-resource ASR lacks labeled data, while purely supervised transfer ignores abundant unlabeled target audio and purely self-supervised pretraining may not learn phoneme-aligned, SR-specific features that transfer well across languages and domains.
   method:  Pretrain a wav2vec 2.0-style encoder with a unified multitask objective that combines supervised phonetic CTC and phonetically-aware contrastive self-supervision. On labeled source data, jointly optimize CTC to align contextual representations with phoneme labels and a masked contrastive loss between contextual embeddings and quantized latent targets; on unlabeled target data, apply the same contrastive objective to adapt the codebook and contextual space. The architecture uses a convolutional feature encoder, a Transformer context network, and a Gumbel-softmax vector quantizer with codebook diversity regularization, followed by fine-tuning (freezing the feature encoder) on small labeled target sets.

19. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Robotic policies must fuse asynchronous, multi-frequency sensory streams (e.g., sparse images with high-rate proprioception/forces) and act nearly in continuous time. Common approaches batch observations into fixed windows or use open-loop primitives, failing to capture fast in-between dynamics and temporal structure.
   method:  Introduce InFuser, a hybrid continuous-time policy that treats control as the solution of a Neural Controlled Differential Equation. The model conditions on an image embedding and is driven by the higher-frequency sensor stream to evolve a continuous-time multisensory latent state, from which actions are decoded at any intermediate time. The initial action uses only currently available measurements, while subsequent actions integrate new high-rate observations via the CDE. Training uses adjoint-based backpropagation and behavior cloning over control functions. The architecture includes passing high-frequency measurements as a subvector of the latent and stacking an identity block in the CDE vector field, and it generalizes ODE/RNN/DMP parameterizations.

20. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

21. [ROBOTICS] Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning (1708.04225)
   problem: Many manipulation tasks depend on multiple objects whose relative positions matter, requiring perception that can jointly localize several task-relevant items and feed a compact state to the policy.
   method:  Stack multiple attention vectors in W to attend to several objects simultaneously over the shared meta-attention proposals and semantic features. Learn these attentions from a small set of kinesthetic demonstrations (initialized with example crops), then feed the attended boxes to a guided policy search controller. Demonstrated on a sweeping task (orange into dustpan) with varying object positions, achieving strong performance and robustness to distractors.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.
