1. Dynamic Swin Transformer With Early Exit for Efficient Quality Defect Detection in Power Distribution Network (IEEE Access 2025, cites: 1)
   Operational reliability of energy metering devices is paramount for modern smart grids, yet these devices face multifaceted degradation from concurrent environmental, electrical, and mechanical stresses. Conventional diagnostic methods prove inadequate in effectively integrating heterogeneous multi-modal sensor data for comprehensive fault prognostics. The “intelligent confidence-based early-exit mechanism” is defined as an adaptive decision system that dynamically adjusts the computational depth of a neural network by evaluating the confidence levels of intermediate layer features in real time, combined with KL-divergence verification, to allocate computational resources on demand while ensuring detection accuracy. To address this, we introduce a Hybrid Deep Network (HDN) featuring modality-specific feature extractors, an adaptive cross-modal attention fusion mechanism, and a dual-output diagnostic module. For industrial quality inspection, we further develop a Dynamic Swin Transformer Network (DTN) incorporating an intelligent confidence-based early-exit mechanism. The DTN dynamically selects optimal computational pathways conditioned on defect characteristics, enabling efficient inference via conditional computation. Extensive evaluations on the curated PowerDefect dataset (12,500 thermal/visible images across 15 defect categories) demonstrate that the DTN achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy of 98.7% mAP@0.5, representing a +1.9% improvement over static Swin Transformers, while simultaneously reducing computational load by 31.4% (6.6 GFLOPs). Crucially, the early-exit mechanism facilitates real-time inference at 86 FPS—a $3.2\times $ speedup—with 79.3% of samples exiting early without accuracy degradation. This framework delivers a practical, high-efficiency solution for smart grid monitoring and industrial inspection, with significant reductions in unplanned maintenance validated in European utility deployments. The architecture’s adaptability also suggests promising extensions to diverse material defect detection scenarios. Future work will focus on adaptive threshold optimization and edge-computing implementations for large-scale distributed systems.

2. A Dynamic Transformer Network With Early Exit Mechanism for Fast Detection of Multiscale Surface Defects (IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement 2023, cites: 10)
   Efficiency of surface defect detection is largely improved with the application of convolutional neural networks (CNNs). However, CNNs encounter difficulties in accurately modeling the multiscale features of defects due to the inherent locality of convolution. Additionally, existing methods for cross-level feature fusion introduce cumbersome computational complexity. Consequently, current networks struggle to reconcile location accuracy of multiscale defects with detection efficiency, resulting in poor performance in real-time detection. To this end, we proposed a dynamic transformer network for surface defect detection, utilizing self-attention-based transformers to precisely capture long-range semantic information within a routing space. Meanwhile, the network dynamically selected feature fusion paths according to inputs, enhancing its ability to aggregate multiscale defect features. Additionally, an early exit mechanism was designed by introducing a multitask loss function to induce defect-free inputs to exit from shallow layers of the network, thereby improving the detection speed. Furthermore, a novel learning algorithm was proposed with a consistent prediction loss function to strengthen the hierarchical relationship between the classification task and segmentation task. By performing experiments on self-built steel surface dataset with multiscale defects, the proposed framework outperformed all competing models in terms of detection accuracy and inference speed, demonstrating that the proposed network can be used for online accurate detection for multiscale surface defects.

3. Multi-Exit Vision Transformer for Dynamic Inference (British Machine Vision Conference 2021, cites: 42)
   Deep neural networks can be converted to multi-exit architectures by inserting early exit branches after some of their intermediate layers. This allows their inference process to become dynamic, which is useful for time critical IoT applications with stringent latency requirements, but with time-variant communication and computation resources. In particular, in edge computing systems and IoT networks where the exact computation time budget is variable and not known beforehand. Vision Transformer is a recently proposed architecture which has since found many applications across various domains of computer vision. In this work, we propose seven different architectures for early exit branches that can be used for dynamic inference in Vision Transformer backbones. Through extensive experiments involving both classification and regression problems, we show that each one of our proposed architectures could prove useful in the trade-off between accuracy and speed.

4. FIANCEE: Faster Inference of Adversarial Networks via Conditional Early Exits (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2023, cites: 4)
   Generative DNNs are a powerful tool for image synthesis, but they are limited by their computational load. On the other hand, given a trained model and a task, e.g. faces generation within a range of characteristics, the output image quality will be unevenly distributed among images with different characteristics. It follows, that we might restrain the model's complexity on some instances, maintaining a high quality. We propose a method for diminishing computations by adding so-called early exit branches to the original architecture, and dynamically switching the computational path depending on how difficult it will be to render the output. We apply our method on two different SOTA models performing generative tasks: generation from a semantic map, and cross-reenactment of face expressions; showing it is able to output images with custom lower-quality thresholds. For a threshold of LPIPS ≤ 0.1, we diminish their computations by up to a half. This is especially relevant for real-time applications such as synthesis of faces, when quality loss needs to be contained, but most of the inputs need fewer computations than the complex instances.

5. Efficient Batched Inference in Conditional Neural Networks (IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems 2024, cites: 1)
   Conditional neural networks (NNs) are networks in which the computations performed vary based on the input. Many NNs of interest (such as autoregressive transformers for sequence generation tasks) are inherently conditional since they process variable-length inputs or produce variable-length outputs. In addition, popular NN optimization techniques, such as early exit, result in the computational footprint varying across inputs. Computational irregularity across inputs presents a challenge to batching, a technique widely used to improve hardware utilization and throughput during NN inference. To address this challenge, we propose BatchCond, an optimized batching framework for Conditional NNs that consists of two key steps: 1) computational similarity-driven batching (SimBatch) and 2) adaptive batch reorganization (ABR). SimBatch utilizes a lightweight DNN predictor to create batches of inputs that are more likely to share similar computational patterns, thereby reducing computational irregularity. Further, ABR addresses residual irregularity by dynamically splitting batches into computationally similar sub-batches in a hardware-aware manner. Our experiments demonstrate that BatchCond improves the overall throughput of batched inference by up to $6.6\times $ (mean of $2.5\times $ ) across a suite of diverse Conditional NNs, including early-exit networks, dynamic slimmable networks, and autoregressive transformers. Code is available at https://github.com/surya00060/BatchCond.

6. A Novel Convolutional Vision Transformer Network for Effective Level-of-Detail Awareness in Digital Twins (Electronics 2025, cites: 0)
   In this paper, we propose a novel integrated model architecture, called a level-of-detail (LoD)-aware convolutional vision transformer network (LCvT). It is designed to enhance digital twin (DT) synchronization by effectively integrating LoD awareness in DTs through hierarchical image classification. LCvT employs a vision transformer (ViT)-based backbone coupled with dedicated branch networks for each LoD. This integration of ViT and branch networks ensures that key features are accurately detected and tailored to the specific objectives of each detail level while also efficiently extracting common features across all levels. Furthermore, LCvT leverages a coarse-to-fine inference strategy and incorporates an early exit mechanism for each LoD, which significantly reduces computational overhead without compromising accuracy. This design enables a single model to dynamically adapt to varying LoD requirements in real-time, offering substantial improvements in inference time and resource efficiency compared to deploying separate models for each level. Through extensive experiments on benchmark datasets, we demonstrate that LCvT outperforms existing methods in accuracy and efficiency across all LoDs, especially in DT synchronization scenarios where LoD requirements fluctuate dynamically.

7. System-1.5 Reasoning: Traversal in Language and Latent Spaces with Dynamic Shortcuts (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 14)
   Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning enables large language models (LLMs) to move beyond fast System-1 responses and engage in deliberative System-2 reasoning. However, this comes at the cost of significant inefficiency due to verbose intermediate output. Recent latent-space reasoning methods improve efficiency by operating on hidden states without decoding into language, yet they treat all steps uniformly, failing to distinguish critical deductions from auxiliary steps and resulting in suboptimal use of computational resources. In this paper, we propose System-1.5 Reasoning, an adaptive reasoning framework that dynamically allocates computation across reasoning steps through shortcut paths in latent space. Specifically, System-1.5 Reasoning introduces two types of dynamic shortcuts. The model depth shortcut (DS) adaptively reasons along the vertical depth by early exiting non-critical tokens through lightweight adapter branches, while allowing critical tokens to continue through deeper Transformer layers. The step shortcut (SS) reuses hidden states across the decoding steps to skip trivial steps and reason horizontally in latent space. Training System-1.5 Reasoning involves a two-stage self-distillation process: first distilling natural language CoT into latent-space continuous thought, and then distilling full-path System-2 latent reasoning into adaptive shortcut paths (System-1.5 Reasoning). Experiments on reasoning tasks demonstrate the superior performance of our method. For example, on GSM8K, System-1.5 Reasoning achieves reasoning performance comparable to traditional CoT fine-tuning methods while accelerating inference by over 20x and reducing token generation by 92.31% on average.

8. MT-DyNN: Multi-Teacher Distilled Dynamic Neural Network for Instance-Adaptive Detection in Autonomous Driving (IEEE transactions on intelligent transportation systems (Print) 2025, cites: 1)
   Multi-object detection in autonomous driving faces challenges due to multi-scale entities, diverse streetscapes, and limited computational resources. To address these challenges, we present MT-DyNN, a Multi-Teacher knowledge-distilled Dynamic Neural Network framework for instance-adaptive detection, optimizing detection accuracy and inference cost in autonomous driving. The framework’s student network comprises a customizable multi-branch residual detection network and a lightweight policy network. The former efficiently extracts multi-scale features in parallel without altering receptive fields, while the latter, depending on curriculum learning, captures task-relevant features and dynamically generates routing vectors to guide the activation or deactivation of residual blocks according to image instance complexity. The framework’s teacher network employs a soft-voting strategy to consolidate knowledge from multiple pre-trained teacher models, providing consistent guidance to the student. Within this distillation paradigm, the policy network’s routing search space is gradually refined, and the policy and detection networks are jointly fine-tuned to optimize the alignment between routing decisions and feature extraction. Experimental results on CIFAR and ImageNet demonstrate that compared to early exiting and stochastic depth methods, MT-DyNN achieves higher accuracy at the same inference cost and reduces the cost by 50% and 59% at comparable accuracy levels. The generated routing maintains channel sparsity across diverse scenarios.

9. Single-Layer Vision Transformers for More Accurate Early Exits with Less Overhead (Neural Networks 2021, cites: 46)
   Deploying deep learning models in time-critical applications with limited computational resources, for instance in edge computing systems and IoT networks, is a challenging task that often relies on dynamic inference methods such as early exiting. In this paper, we introduce a novel architecture for early exiting based on the vision transformer architecture, as well as a fine-tuning strategy that significantly increase the accuracy of early exit branches compared to conventional approaches while introducing less overhead. Through extensive experiments on image and audio classification as well as audiovisual crowd counting, we show that our method works for both classification and regression problems, and in both single- and multi-modal settings. Additionally, we introduce a novel method for integrating audio and visual modalities within early exits in audiovisual data analysis, that can lead to a more fine-grained dynamic inference.

1. COST-EFF: Collaborative Optimization of Spatial and Temporal Efficiency with Slenderized Multi-exit Language Models (Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2022, cites: 6)
   Transformer-based pre-trained language models (PLMs) mostly suffer from excessive overhead despite their advanced capacity. For resource-constrained devices, there is an urgent need for a spatially and temporally efficient model which retains the major capacity of PLMs. However, existing statically compressed models are unaware of the diverse complexities between input instances, potentially resulting in redundancy and inadequacy for simple and complex inputs. Also, miniature models with early exiting encounter challenges in the trade-off between making predictions and serving the deeper layers. Motivated by such considerations, we propose a collaborative optimization for PLMs that integrates static model compression and dynamic inference acceleration. Specifically, the PLM is slenderized in width while the depth remains intact, complementing layer-wise early exiting to speed up inference dynamically. To address the trade-off of early exiting, we propose a joint training approach that calibrates slenderization and preserves contributive structures to each exit instead of only the final layer. Experiments are conducted on GLUE benchmark and the results verify the Pareto optimality of our approach at high compression and acceleration rate with 1/8 parameters and 1/19 FLOPs of BERT.

2. Sorted LLaMA: Unlocking the Potential of Intermediate Layers of Large Language Models for Dynamic Inference (Findings 2023, cites: 8)
   Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing (NLP) by excelling at understanding and generating human-like text. However, their widespread deployment can be prohibitively expensive. SortedNet is a recent training technique for enabling dynamic inference by leveraging the modularity in networks and sorting sub-models based on computation/accuracy in a nested manner. We extend SortedNet to generative NLP tasks, making large language models dynamic without any Pre-Training and by only replacing Standard Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Sorted Fine-Tuning (SoFT). Our approach boosts model efficiency, eliminating the need for multiple models for various scenarios during inference. We show that this approach can unlock the potential of intermediate layers of transformers in generating the target output. Our sub-models remain integral components of the original model, minimizing storage requirements and transition costs between different computational/latency budgets. The efficacy of our proposed method was demonstrated by applying it to tune LLaMA 2 13B on the Stanford Alpaca dataset for instruction following and TriviaQA for closed-book question answering. Our results show the superior performance of sub-models in comparison to Standard Fine-Tuning and SFT+ICT (Early-Exit), all achieved with very efficient tuning and without additional memory usage during inference.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?


### Proposed method
We propose Conditional Subnetwork Activation (CSA), a single model trained to expose multiple, input-dependent compute paths at inference. Each backbone block contains two parallel branches: a light, local-processing path (e.g., gated MLP or depthwise-conv MLP) and a heavier, global-context path (e.g., attention). A tiny router (shared across tokens or operating on token groups) chooses which branch(es) to execute per block given a cheap summary of the current hidden state. During training, we apply stochastic branch dropout to force both branches to be individually competent and to encourage graceful degradation when only one is active. We add a soft FLOPs budget regularizer on the router’s expected choices so the model learns to allocate compute where it pays off most.

To avoid running unnecessary depth, we attach auxiliary prediction heads at several layers and adopt a dynamic early-exit rule driven by layer-wise saturation: compute the cosine similarity between consecutive hidden states; when similarity exceeds a calibrated threshold and the corresponding head’s confidence is sufficient, we stop and emit the output. For autoregressive generation, we decay the threshold across decoding steps and cap minimum layers to avoid premature exits on early tokens. This turns depth into an on-demand resource, activating more layers only for difficult inputs or steps.

Within each block, we further make width conditional by nesting smaller channel slices inside larger ones. Linear and attention projections are parameterized in a Matryoshka style so the first w% of channels approximate a competent subnetwork and larger widths strictly extend it. We implement this as masked matrix multiplications/grouped projections that compute only the selected slice, avoiding kernel recompilations. Training samples widths per batch (e.g., 25/50/100%), distills from the full-width path to narrower slices, and jointly optimizes the task loss so all widths remain aligned.

At inference, CSA follows a progressive schedule that minimizes overhead: start with a narrow width and only the light branch for a small number of layers; if the saturation metric indicates underfit or the head is uncertain, escalate compute by enabling the heavy branch and/or increasing width for subsequent layers; otherwise, early-exit. Routers operate with hysteresis and a global budget controller to stabilize decisions and respect latency targets. Optionally, a lightweight token scorer can route only the top-p tokens through the heavy branch while the remainder stay on the light path, providing additional sparsity without architectural changes.

### Rationale
Dense models waste compute by executing the full depth, width, and global-context machinery on every input. CSA reduces all three dimensions adaptively: branch routing decides whether to pay for global context per block; early exiting truncates depth once representations saturate; and Matryoshka-style slicing executes only the necessary width. Because branches are trained with dropout and slices are co-trained with distillation, any activated subnetwork remains well-formed, limiting accuracy loss under reduced compute.

The key is to couple low-cost, reliable signals to gating. Layer-wise cosine similarity provides a model-internal saturation proxy that generalizes across tasks and supports safe early exit. Branch dropout makes per-branch activation robust, enabling per-input routing without retraining. Nested weight slicing turns width into a knob the router can adjust on-the-fly using the same backbone, avoiding multiple models. Together, these mechanisms conditionally activate only the relevant subnetwork for each input and deliver substantial inference savings with bounded quality impact.

1. Dynamic Swin Transformer With Early Exit for Efficient Quality Defect Detection in Power Distribution Network (IEEE Access 2025, cites: 1)
   Operational reliability of energy metering devices is paramount for modern smart grids, yet these devices face multifaceted degradation from concurrent environmental, electrical, and mechanical stresses. Conventional diagnostic methods prove inadequate in effectively integrating heterogeneous multi-modal sensor data for comprehensive fault prognostics. The “intelligent confidence-based early-exit mechanism” is defined as an adaptive decision system that dynamically adjusts the computational depth of a neural network by evaluating the confidence levels of intermediate layer features in real time, combined with KL-divergence verification, to allocate computational resources on demand while ensuring detection accuracy. To address this, we introduce a Hybrid Deep Network (HDN) featuring modality-specific feature extractors, an adaptive cross-modal attention fusion mechanism, and a dual-output diagnostic module. For industrial quality inspection, we further develop a Dynamic Swin Transformer Network (DTN) incorporating an intelligent confidence-based early-exit mechanism. The DTN dynamically selects optimal computational pathways conditioned on defect characteristics, enabling efficient inference via conditional computation. Extensive evaluations on the curated PowerDefect dataset (12,500 thermal/visible images across 15 defect categories) demonstrate that the DTN achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy of 98.7% mAP@0.5, representing a +1.9% improvement over static Swin Transformers, while simultaneously reducing computational load by 31.4% (6.6 GFLOPs). Crucially, the early-exit mechanism facilitates real-time inference at 86 FPS—a $3.2\times $ speedup—with 79.3% of samples exiting early without accuracy degradation. This framework delivers a practical, high-efficiency solution for smart grid monitoring and industrial inspection, with significant reductions in unplanned maintenance validated in European utility deployments. The architecture’s adaptability also suggests promising extensions to diverse material defect detection scenarios. Future work will focus on adaptive threshold optimization and edge-computing implementations for large-scale distributed systems.

   Efficiency of surface defect detection is largely improved with the application of convolutional neural networks (CNNs). However, CNNs encounter difficulties in accurately modeling the multiscale features of defects due to the inherent locality of convolution. Additionally, existing methods for cross-level feature fusion introduce cumbersome computational complexity. Consequently, current networks struggle to reconcile location accuracy of multiscale defects with detection efficiency, resulting in poor performance in real-time detection. To this end, we proposed a dynamic transformer network for surface defect detection, utilizing self-attention-based transformers to precisely capture long-range semantic information within a routing space. Meanwhile, the network dynamically selected feature fusion paths according to inputs, enhancing its ability to aggregate multiscale defect features. Additionally, an early exit mechanism was designed by introducing a multitask loss function to induce defect-free inputs to exit from shallow layers of the network, thereby improving the detection speed. Furthermore, a novel learning algorithm was proposed with a consistent prediction loss function to strengthen the hierarchical relationship between the classification task and segmentation task. By performing experiments on self-built steel surface dataset with multiscale defects, the proposed framework outperformed all competing models in terms of detection accuracy and inference speed, demonstrating that the proposed network can be used for online accurate detection for multiscale surface defects.

   Deep neural networks can be converted to multi-exit architectures by inserting early exit branches after some of their intermediate layers. This allows their inference process to become dynamic, which is useful for time critical IoT applications with stringent latency requirements, but with time-variant communication and computation resources. In particular, in edge computing systems and IoT networks where the exact computation time budget is variable and not known beforehand. Vision Transformer is a recently proposed architecture which has since found many applications across various domains of computer vision. In this work, we propose seven different architectures for early exit branches that can be used for dynamic inference in Vision Transformer backbones. Through extensive experiments involving both classification and regression problems, we show that each one of our proposed architectures could prove useful in the trade-off between accuracy and speed.

   Generative DNNs are a powerful tool for image synthesis, but they are limited by their computational load. On the other hand, given a trained model and a task, e.g. faces generation within a range of characteristics, the output image quality will be unevenly distributed among images with different characteristics. It follows, that we might restrain the model's complexity on some instances, maintaining a high quality. We propose a method for diminishing computations by adding so-called early exit branches to the original architecture, and dynamically switching the computational path depending on how difficult it will be to render the output. We apply our method on two different SOTA models performing generative tasks: generation from a semantic map, and cross-reenactment of face expressions; showing it is able to output images with custom lower-quality thresholds. For a threshold of LPIPS ≤ 0.1, we diminish their computations by up to a half. This is especially relevant for real-time applications such as synthesis of faces, when quality loss needs to be contained, but most of the inputs need fewer computations than the complex instances.

5. Efficient Batched Inference in Conditional Neural Networks (IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems 2024, cites: 1)
   Conditional neural networks (NNs) are networks in which the computations performed vary based on the input. Many NNs of interest (such as autoregressive transformers for sequence generation tasks) are inherently conditional since they process variable-length inputs or produce variable-length outputs. In addition, popular NN optimization techniques, such as early exit, result in the computational footprint varying across inputs. Computational irregularity across inputs presents a challenge to batching, a technique widely used to improve hardware utilization and throughput during NN inference. To address this challenge, we propose BatchCond, an optimized batching framework for Conditional NNs that consists of two key steps: 1) computational similarity-driven batching (SimBatch) and 2) adaptive batch reorganization (ABR). SimBatch utilizes a lightweight DNN predictor to create batches of inputs that are more likely to share similar computational patterns, thereby reducing computational irregularity. Further, ABR addresses residual irregularity by dynamically splitting batches into computationally similar sub-batches in a hardware-aware manner. Our experiments demonstrate that BatchCond improves the overall throughput of batched inference by up to $6.6\times $ (mean of $2.5\times $ ) across a suite of diverse Conditional NNs, including early-exit networks, dynamic slimmable networks, and autoregressive transformers. Code is available at https://github.com/surya00060/BatchCond.

   In this paper, we propose a novel integrated model architecture, called a level-of-detail (LoD)-aware convolutional vision transformer network (LCvT). It is designed to enhance digital twin (DT) synchronization by effectively integrating LoD awareness in DTs through hierarchical image classification. LCvT employs a vision transformer (ViT)-based backbone coupled with dedicated branch networks for each LoD. This integration of ViT and branch networks ensures that key features are accurately detected and tailored to the specific objectives of each detail level while also efficiently extracting common features across all levels. Furthermore, LCvT leverages a coarse-to-fine inference strategy and incorporates an early exit mechanism for each LoD, which significantly reduces computational overhead without compromising accuracy. This design enables a single model to dynamically adapt to varying LoD requirements in real-time, offering substantial improvements in inference time and resource efficiency compared to deploying separate models for each level. Through extensive experiments on benchmark datasets, we demonstrate that LCvT outperforms existing methods in accuracy and efficiency across all LoDs, especially in DT synchronization scenarios where LoD requirements fluctuate dynamically.

   Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning enables large language models (LLMs) to move beyond fast System-1 responses and engage in deliberative System-2 reasoning. However, this comes at the cost of significant inefficiency due to verbose intermediate output. Recent latent-space reasoning methods improve efficiency by operating on hidden states without decoding into language, yet they treat all steps uniformly, failing to distinguish critical deductions from auxiliary steps and resulting in suboptimal use of computational resources. In this paper, we propose System-1.5 Reasoning, an adaptive reasoning framework that dynamically allocates computation across reasoning steps through shortcut paths in latent space. Specifically, System-1.5 Reasoning introduces two types of dynamic shortcuts. The model depth shortcut (DS) adaptively reasons along the vertical depth by early exiting non-critical tokens through lightweight adapter branches, while allowing critical tokens to continue through deeper Transformer layers. The step shortcut (SS) reuses hidden states across the decoding steps to skip trivial steps and reason horizontally in latent space. Training System-1.5 Reasoning involves a two-stage self-distillation process: first distilling natural language CoT into latent-space continuous thought, and then distilling full-path System-2 latent reasoning into adaptive shortcut paths (System-1.5 Reasoning). Experiments on reasoning tasks demonstrate the superior performance of our method. For example, on GSM8K, System-1.5 Reasoning achieves reasoning performance comparable to traditional CoT fine-tuning methods while accelerating inference by over 20x and reducing token generation by 92.31% on average.

   Multi-object detection in autonomous driving faces challenges due to multi-scale entities, diverse streetscapes, and limited computational resources. To address these challenges, we present MT-DyNN, a Multi-Teacher knowledge-distilled Dynamic Neural Network framework for instance-adaptive detection, optimizing detection accuracy and inference cost in autonomous driving. The framework’s student network comprises a customizable multi-branch residual detection network and a lightweight policy network. The former efficiently extracts multi-scale features in parallel without altering receptive fields, while the latter, depending on curriculum learning, captures task-relevant features and dynamically generates routing vectors to guide the activation or deactivation of residual blocks according to image instance complexity. The framework’s teacher network employs a soft-voting strategy to consolidate knowledge from multiple pre-trained teacher models, providing consistent guidance to the student. Within this distillation paradigm, the policy network’s routing search space is gradually refined, and the policy and detection networks are jointly fine-tuned to optimize the alignment between routing decisions and feature extraction. Experimental results on CIFAR and ImageNet demonstrate that compared to early exiting and stochastic depth methods, MT-DyNN achieves higher accuracy at the same inference cost and reduces the cost by 50% and 59% at comparable accuracy levels. The generated routing maintains channel sparsity across diverse scenarios.

Through extensive experiments on image and audio classification as well as audiovisual crowd counting, we show that our method works for both classification and regression problems, and in both single- and multi-modal settings. Additionally, we introduce a novel method for integrating audio and visual modalities within early exits in audiovisual data analysis, that can lead to a more fine-grained dynamic inference.

1. COST-EFF: Collaborative Optimization of Spatial and Temporal Efficiency with Slenderized Multi-exit Language Models (Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2022, cites: 6)
   Transformer-based pre-trained language models (PLMs) mostly suffer from excessive overhead despite their advanced capacity. For resource-constrained devices, there is an urgent need for a spatially and temporally efficient model which retains the major capacity of PLMs. However, existing statically compressed models are unaware of the diverse complexities between input instances, potentially resulting in redundancy and inadequacy for simple and complex inputs. Also, miniature models with early exiting encounter challenges in the trade-off between making predictions and serving the deeper layers. Motivated by such considerations, we propose a collaborative optimization for PLMs that integrates static model compression and dynamic inference acceleration. Specifically, the PLM is slenderized in width while the depth remains intact, complementing layer-wise early exiting to speed up inference dynamically. To address the trade-off of early exiting, we propose a joint training approach that calibrates slenderization and preserves contributive structures to each exit instead of only the final layer. Experiments are conducted on GLUE benchmark and the results verify the Pareto optimality of our approach at high compression and acceleration rate with 1/8 parameters and 1/19 FLOPs of BERT.

However, their widespread deployment can be prohibitively expensive. SortedNet is a recent training technique for enabling dynamic inference by leveraging the modularity in networks and sorting sub-models based on computation/accuracy in a nested manner. We extend SortedNet to generative NLP tasks, making large language models dynamic without any Pre-Training and by only replacing Standard Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Sorted Fine-Tuning (SoFT). Our approach boosts model efficiency, eliminating the need for multiple models for various scenarios during inference. We show that this approach can unlock the potential of intermediate layers of transformers in generating the target output. Our sub-models remain integral components of the original model, minimizing storage requirements and transition costs between different computational/latency budgets. The efficacy of our proposed method was demonstrated by applying it to tune LLaMA 2 13B on the Stanford Alpaca dataset for instruction following and TriviaQA for closed-book question answering. Our results show the superior performance of sub-models in comparison to Standard Fine-Tuning and SFT+ICT (Early-Exit), all achieved with very efficient tuning and without additional memory usage during inference.

   Pre-training has proven effective in addressing data scarcity and performance limitations in solving PDE problems with neural operators. However, challenges remain due to the heterogeneity of PDE datasets in equation types, which leads to high errors in mixed training. Additionally, dense pre-training models that scale parameters by increasing network width or depth incur significant inference costs. To tackle these challenges, we propose a novel Mixture-of-Experts Pre-training Operator Transformer (MoE-POT), a sparse-activated architecture that scales parameters efficiently while controlling inference costs. Specifically, our model adopts a layer-wise router-gating network to dynamically select 4 routed experts from 16 expert networks during inference, enabling the model to focus on equation-specific features. Meanwhile, we also integrate 2 shared experts, aiming to capture common properties of PDE and reduce redundancy among routed experts. The final output is computed as the weighted average of the results from all activated experts. We pre-train models with parameters from 30M to 0.5B on 6 public PDE datasets. Our model with 90M activated parameters achieves up to a 40% reduction in zero-shot error compared with existing models with 120M activated parameters. Additionally, we conduct interpretability analysis, showing that dataset types can be inferred from router-gating network decisions, which validates the rationality and effectiveness of the MoE architecture.

To overcome this, we came up with a concept of Dynamic Reasoning Chains through Depth-Specialised Mixture-of-Experts (DS-MoE), a modular framework that extends the Mixture-of-Experts paradigm from width-based to depth-specialised computation. DS-MoE introduces expert modules optimised for distinct reasoning depths—shallow pattern recognition, compositional reasoning, logical inference, memory integration, and meta-cognitive supervision. A learned routing network dynamically assembles custom reasoning chains, activating only the necessary experts to match input complexity. The dataset on which we trained and evaluated DS-MoE is on The Pile, an 800GB corpus covering diverse domains such as scientific papers, legal texts, programming code, and web content, enabling systematic assessment across reasoning depths. Experimental results demonstrate that DSMoE achieves up to 16 per cent computational savings and 35 per cent faster inference compared to uniform-depth transformers, while delivering 2.8 per cent higher accuracy on complex multi-step reasoning benchmarks. Furthermore, routing decisions yield interpretable reasoning chains, enhancing transparency and scalability. These findings establish DS-MoE as a significant advancement in adaptive neural architectures, demonstrating that depth-specialised modular processing can simultaneously improve efficiency, reasoning quality, and interpretability in large-scale language models.

The core part of this paper focuses on various strategies and methods of neural network structure optimization. Firstly, the design principles and effects of the depth, width and dynamic adjustment mechanism, as well as the promoting effect of modular design on information flow and feature selection are discussed in detail. The advantages and application scenarios of typical network structures such as CNN, RNN, LSTM and Transformer in specific tasks are compared and analyzed. Secondly, this paper discusses the application of model compression and acceleration technology in neural network structure optimization in detail, and expounds the weight pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, low-rank decomposition and other model lightweight methods, aiming at reducing the number of parameters and reducing the computational complexity, so as to adapt to the hardware resource constraints and improve the reasoning speed. Thirdly, the paper discusses the value of NAS as an innovative research path in the optimization of neural network structure. Machine learning algorithm is used to automatically explore the optimal network architecture, reduce the burden of manual design, and improve the efficiency of model construction. In addition, this paper also pays attention to the optimization strategies in the training process of neural networks, such as the optimizer's selection learning rate scheduling strategy, early stop rule, etc. These strategies can affect the convergence speed and final performance of the model, and are an indispensable part of the structure optimization. Finally, the paper looks forward to the future trends and challenges of neural network structure optimization, and points out that it should be further combined with hardware development, new models inspired by interdisciplinary knowledge and reinforcement learning and other advanced technologies to promote neural network structure optimization to a higher level. The purpose of this paper is to discuss how to optimize neural network structure effectively to improve its performance, efficiency and generalization ability under the background of current deep learning technology. This paper comprehensively reviews the status quo, key technologies and future trends of neural network structure optimization under the background of deep learning, aiming to provide valuable reference and inspiration for theoretical research and practical application in related fields.

5. Adaptive Semantic Token Selection for AI-native Goal-oriented Communications (2024 IEEE Globecom Workshops (GC Wkshps) 2024, cites: 9)
   In this paper, we propose a novel design for AI-native goal-oriented communications, exploiting transformer neural networks under dynamic inference constraints on communication load and computation. Transformers have become the standard architecture for pre-training large-scale vision and text models, and preliminary results have shown promising performance also in deep joint source-channel coding (JSCC). Here, we consider a dynamic model where communication happens over a channel with variable communication constraints. Leveraging recent works on conditional computation, we exploit the structure of the transformer blocks and the multi-head attention operator to design a trainable semantic token selection mechanism that learns to select relevant tokens (e.g., image patches) from the input signal. This is done dynamically, on a per-input basis, with a rate that can be chosen as an additional input by the user. We show that our model improves over state-of-the-art token selection mechanisms, exhibiting high accuracy for a wide range of latency and communication bottleneck constraints, without the need for deploying multiple architectures tailored to each constraint. Additionally, the proposed token selection mechanism extracts powerful semantics that are easy to understand and explain, paving the way for interpretable-by-design models for the next generation of AI-native communication systems.

6. Structured Pruning Method for Large Language Models with Adaptive Compression Ratios (Artificial Intelligence 2025, cites: 0)
   The article addresses the important challenge of deploying large language models (LLMs) on resource-constrained devices. We analyze the evolution of neural network pruning methods from classical approaches (Optimal Brain Damage, Optimal Brain Surgeon) to modern one-shot techniques for LLMs (SparseGPT, Wanda, SliceGPT, 2SSP). The research demonstrates that while unstructured pruning achieves high compression ratios with small quality loss, it fails to provide real size reduction and inference acceleration on standard hardware due to chaotic sparse matrix structures. In contrast, structured pruning methods ensure hardware efficiency by removing entire structural blocks. We propose the Adaptive 2SSP method (modification of the 2SSP method), which combines adaptive compression ratio selection based on block redundancy with two-stage structured pruning: attention block removal (depth pruning) followed by FFN layer neuron removal (width pruning). Experimental validation on Llama-3.2-3B, Llama-2-7B, and Qwen2.5-3B models demonstrates the method's superiority over existing alternatives (GLU Aware Pruning, Dynamic Slicing, original 2SSP). When removing 40% of Llama-3.2-3B parameters, the method maintains perplexity at 26.35 and average benchmark accuracy at 39.57%, representing the best results among compared methods. Hardware efficiency evaluation for Llama-3.2-3B achieved 35.12% reduction in VRAM consumption and 34.78% acceleration in token generation. For Llama-2-7B, a 3.7-fold speedup was obtained at 20% pruning by overcoming VRAM limitations. The results demonstrate that the proposed method provides an optimal balance between compression degree, execution speed, and model quality preservation, making it an effective tool for adapting modern LLMs to deployment on devices with limited computational resources

7. PrivCore: Multiplication-activation co-reduction for efficient private inference (Neural Networks 2025, cites: 2)
   The marriage of deep neural network (DNN) and secure 2-party computation (2PC) enables private inference (PI) on the encrypted client-side data and server-side models with both privacy and accuracy guarantees, coming at the cost of orders of magnitude communication and latency penalties. Prior works on designing PI-friendly network architectures are confined to mitigating the overheads associated with non-linear (e.g., ReLU) operations, assuming other linear computations are free. Recent works have shown that linear convolutions can no longer be ignored and are responsible for the majority of communication in PI protocols. In this work, we present PrivCore, a framework that jointly optimizes the alternating linear and non-linear DNN operators via a careful co-design of sparse Winograd convolution and fine-grained activation reduction, to improve high-efficiency ciphertext computation without impacting the inference precision. Specifically, being aware of the incompatibility between the spatial pruning and Winograd convolution, we propose a two-tiered Winograd-aware structured pruning method that removes spatial filters and Winograd vectors from coarse to fine-grained for multiplication reduction, both of which are specifically optimized for Winograd convolution in a structured pattern. PrivCore further develops a novel sensitivity-based differentiable activation approximation to automate the selection of ineffectual ReLUs and polynomial options. PrivCore also supports the dynamic determination of coefficient-adaptive polynomial replacement to mitigate the accuracy degradation. Extensive experiments on various models and datasets consistently validate the effectiveness of PrivCore, achieving 2.2× communication reduction with 1.8% higher accuracy compared with SENet (ICLR 2023) on CIFAR-100, and 2.0× total communication reduction with iso-accuracy compared with CoPriv (NeurIPS 2023) on ImageNet.

8. Efficient Dynamic Attention 3D Convolution for Hyperspectral Image Classification (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 6)
   Deep neural networks face several challenges in hyperspectral image classification, including insufficient utilization of joint spatial-spectral information, gradient vanishing with increasing depth, and overfitting. To enhance feature extraction efficiency while skipping redundant information, this paper proposes a dynamic attention convolution design based on an improved 3D-DenseNet model. The design employs multiple parallel convolutional kernels instead of a single kernel and assigns dynamic attention weights to these parallel convolutions. This dynamic attention mechanism achieves adaptive feature response based on spatial characteristics in the spatial dimension of hyperspectral images, focusing more on key spatial structures. In the spectral dimension, it enables dynamic discrimination of different bands, alleviating information redundancy and computational complexity caused by high spectral dimensionality. The DAC module enhances model representation capability by attention-based aggregation of multiple convolutional kernels without increasing network depth or width. The proposed method demonstrates superior performance in both inference speed and accuracy, outperforming mainstream hyperspectral image classification methods on the IN, UP, and KSC datasets.

9. SortedNet: A Scalable and Generalized Framework for Training Modular Deep Neural Networks ( 2023, cites: 3)
   Deep neural networks (DNNs) must cater to a variety of users with different performance needs and budgets, leading to the costly practice of training, storing, and maintaining numerous user/task-specific models. There are solutions in the literature to deal with single dynamic or many-in-one models instead of many individual networks; however, they suffer from significant drops in performance, lack of generalization across different model architectures or different dimensions (e.g. depth, width, attention blocks), heavy model search requirements during training, and training a limited number of sub-models. To address these limitations, we propose SortedNet, a generalized and scalable training solution to harness the inherent modularity of DNNs. Thanks to a generalized nested architecture (which we refer as \textit{sorted} architecture in this paper) with shared parameters and its novel update scheme combining random sub-model sampling and a new gradient accumulation mechanism, SortedNet enables the training of sub-models simultaneously along with the training of the main model (without any significant extra training or inference overhead), simplifies dynamic model selection, customizes deployment during inference, and reduces the model storage requirement significantly. The versatility and scalability of SortedNet are validated through various architectures and tasks, including LLaMA, BERT, RoBERTa (NLP tasks), ResNet and MobileNet (image classification) demonstrating its superiority over existing dynamic training methods. For example, we introduce a novel adaptive self-speculative approach based on sorted-training to accelerate large language models decoding. Moreover, SortedNet is able to train 160 sub-models at once, achieving at least 96\% of the original model's performance.

10. Toward Communication–Computation Integrated Architectures for AI-Agent Communication Networks (IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking 2026, cites: 0)
   As communication technologies evolve, the AI agent communication network (ACN) is expected to play a critical role in enabling future wireless services. These networks must meet diverse connectivity demands, provide enhanced intelligence, and provide reliable communication support for AI agents. In this work, we propose an integrated computation-communication (ICC) architecture that jointly addresses baseband signal processing and neural network (NN) inference within a shared computing framework. A unified modeling approach is established by combining multiple-input multiple-output orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (MIMO-OFDM) channel representation with neural network–based application modeling. On the communication side, we derive spectral efficiency and channel capacity expressions that incorporate quantization error, transmit power, and noise, thus characterizing hardware-induced constraints on channel performance. On the computation side, we analyze the model scaling in terms of depth, width, and input resolution. Building upon this framework, we formulate the joint resource allocation challenge as a Markov Decision Process (MDP). We introduce a learning-driven allocation algorithm that uniquely combines Gibbs sampling with a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) based reinforcement learning agent to solve this MDP. This enables the system to dynamically distribute resources across communication and computation, adapting to variations in datasets, Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) regimes, model configurations, and scenario constraints. The proposed approach improves both task completion time and prediction accuracy compared to conventional fixed resource allocation schemes. Theoretical analysis and simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed architecture, highlighting its potential for efficient cross-layer co-design in next-generation ACNs.

Reasoning: The proposed CSA method combines three distinct mechanisms in a unified framework: (1) parallel light/heavy branch routing per block with stochastic branch dropout and FLOPs regularization, (2) early exit via cosine similarity saturation of hidden states with autoregressive-specific threshold decay, and (3) Matryoshka-style nested width slicing with masked matrix multiplications and co-training via distillation. Searches surfaced COST-EFF (width slimming + early exit for PLMs), SortedNet/Sorted LLaMA (nested depth/width sub-models), and MuE (cosine similarity early exit for VLMs) as the closest individual components. However, no paper was found that jointly combines all three dimensions — branch routing (light/heavy parallel paths), cosine-similarity-driven early exit, and Matryoshka width slicing — in a single adaptive inference framework. The combination of parallel branch routing (inspired by Branchformer) with Matryoshka width and cosine-similarity early exit is not present as a joint application in any surfaced paper. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, with searches returning prior work on individual mechanisms but not their combination.

Prior works:
  - COST-EFF (2022): Combines width slimming with multi-exit early exiting for PLMs, but no branch routing or Matryoshka-style nested slicing.
  - SortedNet / Sorted LLaMA (2023): Nested sub-model training across depth/width dimensions with shared parameters, but no parallel branch routing or cosine-similarity exit signal.
  - MuE: You Need Multiple Exiting (2022): Cosine similarity of hidden states as early exit signal for VLMs with autoregressive threshold decay — directly overlaps the early exit component.
  - Branchformer (2022): Parallel light/heavy branch with branch dropout for inference mode switching — directly overlaps the branch routing component.
  - Matryoshka Representation Learning (2022): Nested width/channel slicing with co-training — overlaps the width slicing component.

messages.content:
You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed 2112b9511767 [IR_REC] — "GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
- Method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

### Seed a9647aa8fe5e [IR_REC] — "Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
- Method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

### Seed 3917da5fd88a [LLM_NLP] — "Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
- Method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

### Seed 663d598bb0b9 [CV] — "KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
- Method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

### Seed 6bafdc8c3e0c [ROBOTICS] — "Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?


### Proposed method
We propose Context-Conditioned ProtoGated Subnetworks (C2PS): replace each dense block with a thin, always-on shared trunk in parallel with a pool of E residual experts. A lightweight router activates only the top-k experts per input (or per token for sequence models), so most parameters remain idle for any given example. The trunk preserves a stable, general pathway; the experts provide capacity that is selectively engaged.

Routing is fast and data-aware. Compute a global context embedding once per input (e.g., CLS/mean-pooled features) and feed it to all routers. At each block, the router forms a compact local summary of the incoming features (e.g., per-token or spatially pooled) and scores it against a bank of learned prototypes using cosine similarity with a sharpening nonlinearity. The router fuses local scores with a FiLM-style modulation from the global context to make context-aware, top-k selections. To keep routing overhead low even with larger E, we maintain a streaming top-k via limited-scope competition: only adjacent near-top candidates are compared and swapped until convergence to the k winners, avoiding full O(E^2) pairwise ranking.

Training is end-to-end with standard task loss plus: (1) a load-balancing regularizer to prevent expert collapse; (2) structured group sparsity on expert parameters that encourages specialization and prunes unused paths into clean subnetworks; and (3) a gradual sparsity curriculum that anneals k from dense toward the target budget, stabilizing early learning. We use straight-through or Gumbel-TopK estimators for discrete routing, and distill from a dense teacher (or a late-training snapshot) to mitigate routing noise while experts specialize.

Inference is strictly conditional: for each layer, compute the router, dispatch activations only to the selected experts, and aggregate their outputs (e.g., sum or weighted sum) with the trunk via residual addition. Budgets are adjustable at runtime by tuning k or the routing temperature. For transformers, apply C2PS to MLPs (per-token routing) and optionally to attention heads (per-head routing); for CNNs, gate residual bottlenecks or channel groups. When routing uncertainty is high (e.g., low prototype margin), fall back to a larger k or the trunk-only path, preserving accuracy under tight budgets.

### Rationale
C2PS achieves conditional compute by learning which subnetwork is relevant for each input and only activating that subset. Prototype-based scoring yields sharp, discriminative routing while remaining inexpensive, and the global-context modulation aligns per-token/per-block choices with the sequence- or image-level intent. Group sparsity and a sparsity curriculum shape experts into truly distinct competencies so that activating only a few preserves accuracy. Load balancing avoids overused experts and keeps throughput stable.

Compared to static pruning, C2PS adapts per input and per location (token/region), capturing input heterogeneity without incurring dense FLOPs. Compared to naive MoE, the prototype router is lighter, better regularized, and stabilized by a shared trunk and knowledge distillation, delivering strong accuracy–efficiency trade-offs and controllable latency.

1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.
